item.title: Conflicted
item.content:encoded: I ADORE complicated between the ethics committee and the o5 council. But with this article that is kind of where it ends. Instead of making both sides have their own reasoning and logic, the O5 council is straight up evil. Mentally traumatizing a kid so then they get a super weapon has absolutely nothing to do with securing containing and protecting. Maybe if they trained this kid to contain other scp's, maybe i could get behind it. But This sounds like something the GOC, or some evil goi would do. not the foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I have three observations:

First, “metahuman child studied by unethical science group” is perhaps second only to “attempt to create supersoldiers that went awry” in terms of superhero/villain origins.

Second, I think this is the first time that I’ve realized that the Foundation actually predates the MCU. The age of the “X-Man” SCP is over, let the reign of the “Avenger” SCP begin!

Third, this SCP underscores why people posit that the Ethics Committee has the absolute final say on things: because otherwise, the O-5’s would choose the unethical option every time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2241: What does SCP mean, mister?

Valdez: It means "Secure, Contain, Protect"

SCP-2241: So it fights bad guys? Like SHIELD and the 'vengers?

Valdez: That's right, Cameron. Like the Avengers.

I stopped reading at this point, and immediately upvoted.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Brightburn time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just want to see what happens next!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was rather wholesome and nice and first, and then went right into the sadder, darker parts of a child superhero story. The first time I've ever said 'oh no…' while reading an article. +1

I would like to thank everyone who criticised this piece and helped me make it better, and also myself, who got really sick of people writing superheroes without asking the question "what kind of person would think of themselves as a superhero if anomalous shit started happening to them?" and decided to write a literal x-man Avenger SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this, Excellent work dude! I can even see a tale coming of this about an ethics committee employee battling against the odds to try stop them messing the poor little bugger up any further.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why does O5-3 like this object so much?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something I feel like writers tend to forget* is that the Foundation isn't there to just lock things away; when possible, they want to study these anomalies. This is an example of them doing just that. An impressionable reality bender whom they can manipulate so easily is prime fodder for learning more about how reality benders work, which could, among other things, lead to better ways of securing and containing the more troublesome specimens.

*(or disagree with; obligatory headcanon disclaimer goes here)

I also like the subversion of the "humanoid what can do cool shit and wants to Do Good and is given privileges, etc., etc." template — it's drilled in that the object is very much a test subject and is only indulged to the extent necessary to squeeze results out of it, and regard for its humanity is simply not a priority. It's Foundation cold. While this kind of "taking a bad cliché and turning it around" thing may itself be a cliché by now, well…that doesn't mean I can't like it. *shrugs*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, this was a roller coaster. The reversal on "x-men syndrome anomaly" into "cooperative, gullible test object" was gratifying and well written and the powers progressing with age added an element of uncertainty (what would happen if he grew up and became suspicious or restless? Would an adolescent reality bender have a rude awakening and devastate containment?).

However, the climax of the article felt less plausible to me for reasons already enumerated. It's a serious stretch for me to believe a majority of 05s are willing to ignore the inherent risks in goading a reality bender towards omnipotence while noting that it is becoming unstable. My personal belief is that the foundation would never knowingly attempt to worsen the impact of an anomaly. I think that the test log prior to the escalation was interesting and that there's room for expansion of the logs to note his powers as he ages, but the storyline following what appears to be a singular researcher destabilizing the anomaly with the backing of a single 05 falls just outside of believability to me.

I've never been so confident that I was going to upvote a skip only to get turned around in the final paragraphs. No vote for me, but I appreciate a majority of the writing and if it wasn't so counter to my canon it'd be an exemplar skip.

+ Typos
- Hide typos

"Unconscious" is written as "unconcious" several times in the testing logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: omg he's so cute +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know I'm very late to the game here, but I'm downvoting for a variety of reasons. As TyGently said, both the ethical dilemma and doing-bad-things-for-science angles aren't particularly well developed.

Personally I think the ethical debate should have ended with the phrase "SCP-2241 has become unstable in a manner dangerous to both itself and the Foundation", but eh.

More importantly, the researchers in this are brick stupid. This operates on the level of comic book science, coming nothing near anything remotely realistic.

Details:

+ show block
– hide block

So you want to see how a young reality bender a) manipulates reality and b) develops over time. He believes you're scientists trying to study him to create more superheroes, so he'll probably go along with anything science-y looking short of vivisection.

If you believe his brain is abnormal, where are the tests of his powers while he's strapped into an fMRI, or other neuro-imaging studies? Where are the Trans-Cranial Magnetic Stimulation trials, activating or inhibiting various brain regions? Why in the hell would they jump to invasive neurosurgery (unless it's 1975)?

If you think his powers will change over time, where is the long-term observation and longitudinal analysis? Where's the attempt build a taxonomy and quantitative description of his abilities, rooted in his own descriptions, systematically recording changes over time, rather than hastily and inaccurately jotting them down as they pop up? Why are they in such a hurry that they'll do incredibly unscientific and uninformative tests? What on Earth is faking superhero incidents going to prove? That stress improves his powers? That he can create new powers appropriate to the situation? When you're manipulating dozens of things at once, and never repeat the same conditions, what is your true Independent Variable?

Maybe they're proving that they can turn a child into a violent, unstable, insanely powerful reality bender. That's how they all apparently end up. You've proven (and learned) nothing, unless the Foundation is committed to kidnapping all reality benders as children and doing the exact same things to them.

Edit: I'm not writing this to suggest that all these things should be done; I'm just trying to point out that there are many alternatives to the path they take, such that the path they take is both destructive and meaningless.

So, for me, you've got an ethical argument that was won on the first stroke. Then you've got a research team and the majority of the O5 Council doing horrific things for the advancement of "science," but the science involved is so nonsensical and cartoonish that I can't even begin to take that storyline seriously.

I'm much more tolerant of comic-book science in many contexts, but in this particular kind of parable, the science needs to be worth fighting for. Right now, it just comes off as lolFoundation.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted due to a number of factors, some of which are mentioned by others, all of which I am too tired to explain right now - PM me if this is rewritten and I'll revote accordingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty sure he's being made unstable as it is. The back-and-forth thing seems to be pretty necessary to me. I think this is a good example of exactly why the Ethics Committee exists, why the Foundation doesn't weaponize anomalies, and hints at the disastrous consequences of doing so. To me, this reads almost like an origin to 076.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, although I think there's too many back-and-forth stopping the tests and starting the tests. Maybe just a few would do.

Other than that, don't let Cameron see and/or watch anything related to Deadpool's origins. Making a reality bender mentally unstable and also a lot more powerful? That's a Wade Wilson in the making.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know -Js are not canon, but there can be a test log for them, like with the catapult, it references 682 along with a few others, so it's possible for 1550 to have a test log too sometime.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You make a good point, which other people seem to share, and I've edited it slightly to account for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why are you referencing a non-existent SCP number? Furthermore, -J are not canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well written, and a cool concept interestingly explored. But the whole handling-strategy of this SCiP feels way out of character for the Foundation's ideals. Given the Foundation's size, and knowing how bureaucracy can be a slow-moving process, I can understand how this line of testing could go on for as long as it does. But then at the end…

Testing reinstated by majority Overseer vote following council meeting.

A majority of the 05 council defying the Foundation's main principles in favour of research gains and investigating how to make use of a sapient anomalous humanoid that's in containment… By conditioning this kid to be even more powerful, the Foundation is undermining it's ability to contain the anomaly. By encouraging development of the anomaly, rather than simply preserving it, they act in opposition to their goal of maintaining global normalcy.

I see how gaining understanding of the development of reality benders is beneficial; how, with enough of it, all reality benders could eventually be SCP-EXes. But understanding, for the Foundation, should be secondary to containment and protection.

And they definitely aren't reasonably going to actually use the kid for anything (Omega-7 Jr. in the making) So what happens when you train and condition an attack dog, then just keep it locked in a cage and never let it out to attack? How will a sapient (and naive child, no less) mega-powered reality-bender react if they ever learned the truth about how the Foundation has been manipulating them for most of their lives? It seems like Foundation personnel have just enough comic book knowledge to make an Avengers reference, but "the 'Weapon X' storyline? Never heard of it."

Like I said, it was good. But the final decision from the Overseers coming down the way it did (not for lack-of-ethics reasons, but for defying the Foundation's #1 mission statement) neutralized my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Forgetting the ethical debate for now, would the Foundation really do this, deliberately messing with a potentially dangerous anomaly and trying to make it more powerful and dangerous? That seems like a recipe for disaster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would kind of like to know how long a time we're talking with all of these fairly elaborate murder/rescue tests. It says he's seven now, was he even younger when he was going through all that? Were they just throwing all of those situations at him like every other day?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree, this isn't perfect, but I kind of love it. A humanoid skip who's just beginning to develop reality-bending is something I haven't seen before. The interview was great (though you've got a couple instances of "Interviewer" in there). There are some bits I could niggle at, but I'm sure the curmudgeons will poke those for me. I just like the whole "seem to buy into his superhero complex" thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ For your pleasure
- For your pleasure

O5 Council: Valdez, you're fired.

Valdez: What? No!

O5 Council: Yes. Get out.

Valdez: Fi-

O5 Council: Valdez, we need you. Get back in here.

Valdez: Okay, I, uh, guess I'll go ahead with these tests th-

O5 Council: You'll do nothing of the sort. Get out of here.

Valdez: Aw, okay.

O5 Council: Just kidding, come back.

Valdez: Uh, alright?

O5 Council: Sorry about all that. How about a hug to make up for it?

Valdez: I don't think I should…

O5 Council: Come on, you afraid of hugs?

Valdez: I… guess not.

O5 Council: That's it, bring it in here.

Valdez: I guess this isn't so bad.

O5 Council: Valdez?

Valdez: Yeah?

[The O5 Council suplexes Valdez messily.]

+ Actual Critique
- Actual Critique

On another note, though, I'm not a fan of this article. The plot that goes back and forth between "Foundation doing horrible things for science" and "Foundation having pangs of conscience", but never rises beyond a mediocre take on either.

There was a strong start, but especially after the interview I was constantly confused as to why any of the Foundation personnel consider this to be okay. It's stated that they're doing it for science, but I never understand the actual benefits, and as such the situation seems awfully contrived to produce such a moral dilemma ("We need X! But we can only get X by doing horrible action Y, because that's just how X works.").

And then I'm further confused by wondering exactly how they're doing this. Are they taking this kid out into open fields and telling him there's crime going on? Or are we building elaborate sets and pretending crime only happens within this single site? They fired a mortar at him from long range, so I guess he was just loitering outside (speaking of that, how was that not considered a huge risk to his person)? Did they push a guy down the stairs, or did they wait until someone slipped?

They build a bank vault, had him steal from it, and then noted that he was okay with stealing from it? How in the world is such a test even conceived of? Were they testing his morality? Are there not way less expensive ways to do that than building a bank vault?

And besides all of these hangups, I'm still mystified as to how testing this thing and making it more powerful is an acceptable risks. It doesn't take a genius to realize that emotional instability is a horrible sign in the superpowered killing machine you're making. Even if you want to keep developing it as a killing machine, wouldn't you want that dealt with first and foremost, so it doesn't, say, kill all of you?

So, it's a plot that could work, and a strong premise, but it combines tons of odd and unexplained actions with a plot that doesn't really go anywhere that previous explorations haven't. The Foundation does bad things for good reasons, sometimes. But when they do it for stupid reasons or in spite of obvious dangers, they just look like douches that like to torture kids.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this scip stands up even with the literal "X-man" approach. In fact, I consider this quite clever in the way it utilizes these tropes. Ethics in the Foundation is an extremely fruitful topic with certain articles, and this uses it well; sort of a realistic approach to SCP-5308-J-5.

I could see this evolving into interesting stories about "Cameron the Crusader." Both about his Superhero career and the horrific outcome of his treatment by the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can't wait for SCP-15550-J to have a cross test log. This one would be my entry for a test. Lol made me think of that instantly +1.

EDIT: I mean 1550.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one walks a razor, but ultimately I find myself an upvote. Agree partly with psul's suggestion that the last addendum is a bit heavy handed and redundant to the rest, but I would instead suggest paring it down to just the last two entries about halting and resuming testing (including removing the bit about the exact nature of the proposed test that triggered the halt order.)

My upvote boils down to one thing, and I'm sure that some will downvote for the same reason that I'm upvoting: this reminds me of SCP-239. They may rationalize it as research or containment, but somewhere inside the fallible human beings of the foundation want to see this as a second chance: This time they won't lie about what he can do, but instead teach control and discipline while also ascertaining his actual limits. They'll explain as honestly as they can in terms he can relate to what the Foundation is, what it does, and why it's important. They'll do their best to instill loyalty to the Foundation's cause. And all the while they'll try to ignore that after its all done, they'll still be holding a tiger by the tail if they don't cage it as a cub.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This really, really needs a proofread. There are multiple instances of compound adjectives that aren't hyphenated, a few misspellings and uncapitalized proper nouns, and general structural awkwardness in the sentences. I can go in and fix some of the minor grammatical issues, but the author needs to do the actual proofreading/major fixes.

SCP-2241 has stated that it intends to grow stronger to stop more people from dying, and this will help both it and us in this aim. Killing a man with a teleported bullet was a stroke of tactical genius from the SCP object.

The ethical commentary is heavy-handed throughout this, but this paragraph specifically is where I upvoted.

Edit: I went in and hyphenated the words that needed to be hyphenated, but in reading it twice, this needs a bit of a tonal cleanup as well. A lot of the superhero subject matter and the telekinesis stuff combined with the excessive amounts of personal statements by Foundation departments and personnel is dampening any clinical tone the article had.

Edit 2: I'll note that I left alone the British spellings of words like favorite and whatnot when I fixed a few misspellings.

item.title: Re: This is going to be controversial
item.content:encoded: I think that in general though, just from what I remember seeing, the capabilities of reality benders are pretty poorly defined. When I've seen them used previously they don't tend to have any real limitations and are capable of doing pretty much anything they want to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the ethical debates came across as a little heavy-handed and repetitive by the end. If everything after the testing collapsible was deleted, I would upvote without any other changes.

ETA: the reason for upvoting if the ending was deleted: I quite liked the character and rapport built in the interview, and the fact that it was immediately undermined by the Foundation's testing regime.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I care not for the ethical concerns of the SCP. What that got me to downvote is the interviewer revealing what is the SCP and using terms like "containment" in front of the object being contained.

It would be dangerous to simply let a kid know of the Foundation's name when one can choose not to reply directly or give an alias. Furthermore, terms like "containment" suggest that the SCP is indeed held captive and it is not conducive to cordial relations.

item.title: This is going to be controversial
item.content:encoded:
I'm just calling it now.
I mean, I was pretty conflicted about it myself, but the Ethicist vs the O5 and researcher sealed it as a +1. The entire time I was thinking; "This is clearly destabilizing him mentally, why do they keep testing him?" Then we get to the ethics suspension, and the entire argument setup justifies the sporadic treatment. My one issue is his full skillset being poorly defined after all these tests. It probably could have been left at telekinesis and teleportation. But, ehhh… I'm fine with it.. especially since it feeds my love of quantifying everything with that section on analyzing anomalous brain function.
